WPEA CLEAR PRONUNCIATION SYSTEM — 30 CARDS
CORE 5 — PRIORITY
EXTENDED SOUND SYSTEM
VOWEL CLARITY
DELIVERY & SPEECH CONTROL
WORKPLACE CLARITY APPLICATION
TH SOUND ★
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/θ/   /ð/
Tongue lightly between teeth · Air flows out
think this three they
2. Often Heard As
T  ·  D  ·  F   — changes meaning, signals uncertainty
3. Minimal Pairs
think/tink
then/den
thank/tank
three/tree
this/dis
thin/tin
that/dat
though/dough
they/day
there/der
4. Clarity Sentences
Thanks for taking the time today.
I think we should review this.
This is the third option.
5. Delivery
Clear, not rushed. Let the tongue placement do the work.
6. Self-Check
Did I need to repeat?
50 Common Workplace Words — "TH" Sound
this
thank
anything
thus
those
that
thing
nothing
theory
these
there
three
everything
theme
their
they
third
other
method
there's
them
through
another
author
they're
then
though
either
mother
the
than
thought
neither
father
thirty
think
within
together
brother
thousand
without
whether
throughout
therefore
something
elsewhere
thereafter
themselves
thickness
thorough
R vs L ★
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/r/   /l/
R: tongue back, lips rounded · L: tongue tip to ridge
right light rate late
2. Often Heard As
R→L  ·  L→R   — common in East Asian English · alters key words
3. Minimal Pairs
right/light
rate/late
road/load
red/led
rock/lock
rice/lice
ray/lay
row/low
read/lead
rip/lip
4. Clarity Sentences
The report is ready for review.
Please lead the next release cycle.
We need the real numbers, not last quarter's.
5. Delivery
Slow down on R and L at the start of key business words.
6. Self-Check
Did the word land clearly?
50 Common Workplace Words — R and L Sounds
report
lead
result
level
role
goal
real
legal
risk
link
rate
loyal
ready
allow
revenue
collect
review
global
relate
plan
require
resolve
release
replace
relevant
rely
launch
recall
label
align
resource
reach
learning
regulate
reflect
long
list
rules
large
reliable
respond
client
run
explain
rule
follow
right
local
read
—
V vs B ★
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/v/   /b/
V: top teeth on lower lip, vibrate · B: both lips close and release
value benefit verify best
2. Often Heard As
V→B   — very common · changes value to "balue," vendor to "bendor"
3. Minimal Pairs
very/berry
vest/best
vale/bale
vent/bent
vote/boat
vow/bow
van/ban
vat/bat
veer/beer
vine/bine
4. Clarity Sentences
Verify the vendor contract value.
The budget covers both versions.
We need to validate the business case.
5. Delivery
Feel the friction of V. Don't let it pop like B.
6. Self-Check
Did my lip touch my teeth for V?
50 Common Workplace Words — V and B Sounds
value
vendor
verify
version
viable
volume
valid
visible
variable
validate
budget
benefit
build
brief
beyond
base
board
brand
bridge
broad
deliver
behavior
above
involve
believe
review
save
resolve
observe
approve
provide
prove
move
achieve
improve
available
objective
investment
overview
revenue
problem
advertise
receive
advice
—
—
—
—
—
—
ED ENDINGS ★
Natural Past Tense in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/t/   /d/   /ɪd/
Three sounds — not always said as 'ed' · Rule: follow the base verb sound
worked planned wanted needed
2. Often Heard As
/ɪd/ added to all — "workED," "planNED" · sounds unnatural, signals non-fluency
3. Minimal Pairs
worked /t//walked /t/
planned /d//gained /d/
wanted /ɪd//ended /ɪd/
fixed /t//mixed /t/
moved /d//proved /d/
started /ɪd//landed /ɪd/
helped /t//jumped /t/
closed /d//used /d/
listed /ɪd//tested /ɪd/
reached /t//matched /t/
4. Clarity Sentences
We launched the product and tracked results.
The team planned well and delivered on time.
He wanted feedback but needed more data.
5. Delivery
Match the ED ending to the natural sound that follows the base verb.
6. Self-Check
Which ED sound did that verb need?
50 Common Workplace Words — Past Tense ED Endings
worked
planned
needed
wanted
reviewed
launched
fixed
moved
started
tested
helped
closed
listed
reached
matched
discussed
approved
managed
included
created
completed
delivered
recommended
responded
confirmed
received
handled
scheduled
presented
requested
increased
reduced
updated
shared
signed
changed
improved
decided
provided
tracked
assigned
drafted
measured
reported
finished
submitted
aligned
released
coordinated
supported
FINAL CONSONANTS ★
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/-t/   /-d/   /-s/   /-z/
Don't drop the final sound · It carries meaning and signals fluency
cost need results goals
2. Often Heard As
Word ends too early — "cos" for cost, "nee" for need · changes or loses meaning
3. Minimal Pairs
cost/cos
need/nee
test/tes
find/fin
fact/fac
past/pas
asked/ask
risk/ris
best/bes
mind/min
4. Clarity Sentences
The cost and the risk must both be assessed.
I asked the team to submit by Friday.
Last month's results exceeded our target.
5. Delivery
Give every final consonant a clean, deliberate landing.
6. Self-Check
Did I finish the word completely?
50 Common Workplace Words — Final Consonant Clarity
cost
risk
fact
best
list
test
next
past
last
most
need
lead
find
build
hold
send
read
fund
hand
end
goals
results
details
numbers
reports
updates
issues
process
focus
progress
meets
deadlines
tasks
notes
plans
sales
teams
roles
tools
rules
project
product
market
budget
impact
subject
object
direct
contract
conduct
S ENDINGS
Plural & Third-Person Clarity in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/s/   /z/   /ɪz/
Three sounds — not always /s/ · Follow the base word's final sound
reports goals increases leads
2. Often Heard As
All plurals said as /s/ — "goalS," "leadS" · sounds overly careful or unnatural
3. Minimal Pairs
reports /s//results /s/
goals /z//leads /z/
increases /ɪz//processes /ɪz/
tasks /s//risks /s/
teams /z//problems /z/
reaches /ɪz//launches /ɪz/
costs /s//facts /s/
plans /z//runs /z/
fixes /ɪz//passes /ɪz/
works /s//takes /s/
4. Clarity Sentences
The teams completed all tasks and reviews.
She manages the processes and launches.
He leads five projects across three regions.
5. Delivery
Choose the S sound based on what immediately precedes it in the word.
6. Self-Check
Which S sound fits this word ending?
50 Common Workplace Words — S Endings (Plurals & 3rd Person)
reports
goals
results
tasks
teams
leads
issues
risks
roles
plans
reaches
launches
manages
processes
increases
reviews
phases
cases
resources
focuses
updates
measures
targets
stages
changes
challenges
responses
analyses
approaches
premises
costs
facts
projects
impacts
requests
products
markets
clients
vendors
contacts
accounts
agreements
budgets
records
notes
tests
drafts
assets
sources
benchmarks
W vs V
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/w/   /v/
W: lips rounded, no teeth contact · V: upper teeth on lower lip, vibrate
work value week view
2. Often Heard As
W→V — "vork" for work, "veak" for weak · strong signal of L1 interference
3. Minimal Pairs
work/verge
wine/vine
west/vest
wet/vet
while/vile
way/vague
whale/vale
wear/vary
week/vibe
word/verb
4. Clarity Sentences
We need to work on this week's plan.
The view from vendors is very clear.
What value does this workflow add?
5. Delivery
W never touches the teeth. Feel the difference physically before speaking.
6. Self-Check
Did my teeth stay away from my lip for W?
50 Common Workplace Words — W and V Sounds
work
week
well
while
with
when
were
what
want
will
value
view
vendor
version
verify
valid
viable
volume
vital
various
workflow
overview
deliver
involve
review
above
achieve
improve
observe
receive
forward
reward
awareness
network
software
hardware
midweek
workforce
ownership
always
website
workshop
worldwide
walkthrough
whiteboard
watchlist
withdraw
switch
lower
between
F vs H
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/f/   /h/
F: upper teeth on lower lip, airflow · H: open mouth, breath from throat
focus hire final help
2. Often Heard As
H dropped — "elp" for help, "as" for has · or F→P in some L1 patterns
3. Minimal Pairs
focus/home
final/hire
feel/heel
fill/hill
fat/hat
fit/hit
fog/hog
fan/hand
fold/hold
fact/hacked
4. Clarity Sentences
Help the team focus on the final plan.
Hiring the right fit is a high priority.
Feel free to share feedback at any hour.
5. Delivery
H is breath, not friction. F uses your teeth. Practice both in isolation.
6. Self-Check
Did the H words start with breath, not silence?
50 Common Workplace Words — F and H Sounds
focus
final
follow
feedback
first
finance
forward
full
form
function
hire
help
high
head
hold
handle
has
here
half
history
effort
offer
after
staff
platform
different
effective
conflict
affect
shift
behalf
interface
brief
chief
proof
traffic
refresh
reflect
define
surface
perform
confirm
draft
satisfy
profit
ahead
achieve
behind
inherit
—
S vs SH
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/s/   /ʃ/
S: tongue near front teeth, narrow airflow · SH: tongue back, lips rounded slightly
see she sort short
2. Often Heard As
S→SH or SH→S — "sheet" becomes "seat" in some L1 patterns · causes confusion
3. Minimal Pairs
see/she
seat/sheet
sell/shell
save/shave
sort/short
sip/ship
sign/shine
same/shame
sea/she
sue/shoe
4. Clarity Sentences
Please share the status update by Thursday.
The session starts in the main room.
We should schedule a solution review.
5. Delivery
Contrast consciously. SH is warmer and wider in the mouth than S.
6. Self-Check
Did those two sounds feel different in my mouth?
50 Common Workplace Words — S and SH Sounds
status
session
schedule
solution
system
strategy
service
structure
standard
suggest
share
should
short
show
shape
shift
shutdown
shipment
shortage
showcase
ensure
discuss
resources
business
process
success
assess
access
address
stress
issue
push
finish
publish
establish
measure
pressure
position
mission
commission
decision
discussion
presentation
submission
expression
permission
extension
possession
passion
—
CH vs J
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/tʃ/   /dʒ/
CH: voiceless affricate · J: same shape but voiced (vocal cords vibrate)
change job choose join
2. Often Heard As
CH→SH or J→Y — "shedule" for schedule, "yob" for job in some L1 patterns
3. Minimal Pairs
change/range
cheap/jeep
chain/Jane
chunk/junk
chin/gin
chill/gill
choke/joke
choose/juice
chart/jar
church/judge
4. Clarity Sentences
We need to change the approach for this project.
The challenge is choosing the right tech stack.
Join us for the check-in on Thursday.
5. Delivery
Both sounds stop then release. Voicing is the only difference. Feel it.
6. Self-Check
Did CH feel sharp and J feel buzzy?
50 Common Workplace Words — CH and J Sounds
change
challenge
choice
charge
approach
achieve
each
reach
search
launch
job
project
join
manage
subject
agenda
inject
majority
object
adjective
technology
strategy
adjacent
exchange
checkout
chosen
checkbox
archive
purchase
enriched
advantage
knowledge
acknowledge
region
engine
margin
origin
urgent
suggest
engage
engineer
budget
bridge
language
message
image
package
storage
adjust
—
P vs B
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/p/   /b/
P: voiceless lip stop with puff of air · B: same but voiced, vocal cords vibrate
plan build proposal budget
2. Often Heard As
Final B→P — "job" becomes "jop" · or P→B at word start in some L1 patterns
3. Minimal Pairs
plan/bland
pay/bay
pack/back
pit/bit
pull/bull
push/bush
page/badge
price/brice
point/boil
part/bart
4. Clarity Sentences
The plan and the budget are both pending approval.
Please present the proposal by Friday.
Both products are performing well.
5. Delivery
P has a puff of air. Hold a hand in front of your mouth to feel it.
6. Self-Check
Did P produce a burst of air on my hand?
50 Common Workplace Words — P and B Sounds
plan
project
proposal
process
product
performance
priority
provide
problem
progress
budget
build
brand
board
brief
broad
backup
based
begin
beyond
approach
impact
update
report
support
improve
approve
compete
complete
describe
scope
capability
output
pipeline
template
input
implement
option
export
import
purpose
compare
represent
present
accept
step
stop
group
top
—
T vs D
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/t/   /d/
T: voiceless · D: same tongue position but voiced, vocal cords buzz
target deliver track data
2. Often Heard As
T→D in casual speech — or final D dropped · affects past tense and word-final clarity
3. Minimal Pairs
time/dime
two/do
ten/den
tear/dare
tan/Dan
tick/Dick
tie/die
town/down
tile/dial
try/dry
4. Clarity Sentences
Track the data and deliver the report by Tuesday.
The target date has changed.
Did the team test the new tool?
5. Delivery
T is crisp. D is warm. Final D especially needs a clean voiced landing.
6. Self-Check
Did I give the final D its full voiced value?
50 Common Workplace Words — T and D Sounds
target
track
test
team
tool
task
timeline
training
trend
total
data
deliver
detail
deadline
draft
decision
discuss
direct
document
develop
update
output
product
method
content
context
contact
format
impact
adjust
adopt
accept
conduct
project
standard
department
support
commitment
result
potential
additional
fundamental
technical
external
internal
digital
credential
structural
functional
—
K vs G
Clear Pronunciation in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/k/   /g/
K: back of tongue to soft palate, voiceless · G: same but voiced, buzzing
key goal client global
2. Often Heard As
Final G→K — "league" becomes "leek" · or confusion at word start: "cold" vs "gold"
3. Minimal Pairs
key/go
cap/gap
call/gall
cold/gold
came/game
class/glass
curl/girl
cave/gave
coat/goat
crate/great
4. Clarity Sentences
The key goal is to grow the client base.
Check the global guidelines before committing.
Our core gap is in execution, not knowledge.
5. Delivery
Both K and G form in the back of the mouth. Voicing is the key contrast.
6. Self-Check
Did G have voice and K have just air?
50 Common Workplace Words — K and G Sounds
key
keep
know
check
market
track
make
take
work
document
goal
grow
global
guide
group
manage
engage
change
agree
language
acknowledge
knowledge
background
block
back
black
quick
stock
risk
flag
think
ask
look
book
link
network
package
speak
break
schedule
technique
unique
topic
specific
public
logic
strategic
graphic
organic
—
SHORT vs LONG VOWELS
Vowel Clarity in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/ɪ/ vs /iː/   /ʊ/ vs /uː/
Short vowels are brief and relaxed · Long vowels are sustained and tense
bit beat full fool
2. Often Heard As
Short and long vowels swapped — "sheet" for "shit" (reversed) or "live" for "leave" · causes confusion in meetings
3. Minimal Pairs
bit/beat
sit/seat
ship/sheep
live/leave
fill/feel
full/fool
pull/pool
look/Luke
hit/heat
mill/meal
4. Clarity Sentences
Leave the meeting link in the email.
We need to read the brief before lunch.
This tool will boost team productivity.
5. Delivery
Long vowels need time. Don't rush — the length itself carries meaning.
6. Self-Check
Did I hold the long vowel long enough to be distinct?
50 Common Workplace Words — Short and Long Vowel Clarity
meet
beat
read
leave
feel
deal
speak
reach
clean
lead
bit
sit
ship
live
fill
look
full
pull
put
took
team
meal
seat
real
week
need
keep
deep
sleep
free
list
risk
build
quick
with
grid
skill
still
rich
group
tool
boost
move
prove
choose
rule
cool
pool
school
—
IH vs EE
Vowel Clarity in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/ɪ/   vs   /iː/
/ɪ/ is short, lax, mouth slightly open · /iː/ is long, tense, lips spread
it eat bit beat
2. Often Heard As
Both said the same — "he'll" becomes "hill," or "lead" becomes "lid" · high-stakes errors in meetings
3. Minimal Pairs
it/eat
bit/beat
sit/seat
fit/feet
hit/heat
slip/sleep
ship/sheep
fill/feel
live/leave
rim/ream
4. Clarity Sentences
Please review this list before the meeting.
He will lead the team through the fix.
We need to implement a real system.
5. Delivery
IH is the relaxed version. EE requires deliberate lip tension. Feel the stretch.
6. Self-Check
Did EE feel wider and longer than IH?
50 Common Workplace Words — IH vs EE Vowel Clarity
it
is
in
if
this
with
give
will
risk
build
eat
each
even
easy
email
equal
read
lead
keep
need
meet
deal
team
feel
real
bit
sit
hit
fill
live
beat
seat
feet
heat
sleep
sheep
leave
ream
least
brief
piece
relief
achieve
believe
receive
perceive
repeat
release
agree
—
EH vs AE
Vowel Clarity in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/ɛ/   vs   /æ/
/ɛ/ mid-front vowel (bed) · /æ/ low-front vowel, jaw drops further (bad)
bed bad set sat
2. Often Heard As
Both vowels merged into one — "met" sounds like "mat" or vice versa · affects comprehension of key business terms
3. Minimal Pairs
bed/bad
set/sat
met/mat
send/sand
end/and
pen/pan
ten/tan
went/want
sell/sal
tell/tal
4. Clarity Sentences
We met to plan the next step.
Set a target and track your gap.
Send the draft and add any edits.
5. Delivery
For AE, drop the jaw further and feel the stretch. EH stays higher.
6. Self-Check
Did the jaw drop further for AE than for EH?
50 Common Workplace Words — EH and AE Vowel Clarity
set
step
help
check
best
next
test
send
end
well
plan
track
add
back
task
stand
can
lack
gap
have
met
net
bed
tell
sell
ten
hen
pen
men
sat
bad
mad
had
dad
and
band
land
hand
sand
catch
match
batch
attach
expand
demand
manage
advance
balance
channel
—
UH vs OO
Vowel Clarity in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/ʌ/   vs   /uː/
/ʌ/ central mid vowel (cup) · /uː/ back high vowel, lips round (group)
cup group budget tool
2. Often Heard As
OO used where UH is expected — "boodget" for budget · or UH where OO belongs — "gruhp" for group
3. Minimal Pairs
cup/coop
but/boot
cut/cool
bun/boon
run/rune
dun/dune
luck/Luke
mud/mood
pub/tube
buck/book
4. Clarity Sentences
The budget cuts will affect the whole group.
Run the tool and submit the output.
Our goal is to improve the revenue curve.
5. Delivery
OO requires rounded lips and back tongue position. UH is neutral and central.
6. Self-Check
Did my lips round for OO and stay neutral for UH?
50 Common Workplace Words — UH and OO Vowel Clarity
budget
cut
run
done
result
function
structure
trust
output
discuss
tool
group
improve
move
approve
remove
choose
prove
boost
include
enough
support
month
front
country
company
some
come
above
trouble
continue
conclude
contribute
consume
compute
pursue
excuse
assume
produce
reduce
unique
review
renew
revenue
value
volume
issue
avenue
venue
—
SCHWA
Unstressed Vowel Clarity in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
/ə/
The most common sound in English · Occurs on unstressed syllables · Neutral, brief
a-bout pro-blem a-gree sys-tem
2. Often Heard As
Stressed where schwa belongs — "proBLEM" → "PROBlem" · or full vowel used instead of schwa: sounds formal and unnatural
3. Minimal Pairs
ə-BOUT/A-bout
ə-GREE/AY-gree
SYS-təm/SYS-teem
PRO-bləm/PRO-bloom
ə-DDRESS/AY-dress
BUD-gət/BUD-gate
pro-JECt/PRO-ject
ə-CHIEVE/AY-cheeve
COM-pəny/COM-panee
LEA-dər/LEA-deer
4. Clarity Sentences
The problem with the system is the process.
About the agenda — I agree with the approach.
The leader of the program is available.
5. Delivery
Schwa is the sound of relaxation. Stressed syllables are sharp; schwa is soft.
6. Self-Check
Did unstressed syllables feel quick and neutral?
50 Common Workplace Words — Schwa in Unstressed Syllables
about
above
across
agenda
agree
alone
along
appear
apply
around
available
away
balance
budget
calendar
capable
channel
colleague
company
complete
concern
confirm
consider
consistent
continue
control
deliver
develop
difference
document
external
focus
follow
forward
govern
handle
important
leader
listen
manage
measure
model
moment
normal
offer
often
open
problem
process
program
WORD STRESS
Delivery & Speech Control in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
STRESS
One syllable per word receives the primary beat · Louder, longer, higher in pitch
PRO-ject pro-JECT RE-cord re-CORD
2. Often Heard As
All syllables said equally — sounds robotic and hard to parse · Listener struggles to find the word
3. Minimal Pairs
PRO-ject (noun)/pro-JECT (verb)
RE-cord (noun)/re-CORD (verb)
PER-mit (noun)/per-MIT (verb)
OB-ject (noun)/ob-JECT (verb)
IN-sult (noun)/in-SULT (verb)
CON-duct (noun)/con-DUCT (verb)
CON-tent (noun)/con-TENT (adj)
EX-port (noun)/ex-PORT (verb)
RE-fuse (noun)/re-FUSE (verb)
PRE-sent (noun)/pre-SENT (verb)
4. Clarity Sentences
We need to PRO-ject the budget and re-CORD the results.
Please PRE-sent the pro-JECT timeline.
The PER-mit has been per-MIT-ted.
5. Delivery
Exaggerate stress in practice. In delivery, you'll naturally calibrate back.
6. Self-Check
Could a listener identify the stressed syllable immediately?
50 Common Workplace Words — Word Stress Patterns
PROject
rePORT
DATAbase
aNAlysis
straTEgy
PROgram
deCISion
oBJECtive
LEAdership
comMUNicate
PROcess
imPACT
PERformance
conTRIbute
aCHIEVE
REsource
deVElop
IMplement
STRUCture
asSESS
PRIority
supPORT
INterface
colLABorate
reQUIRE
DELiver
APproach
inCREASE
exPECT
reLEASE
OPtimize
alLOCate
comPETE
enGAGE
EVALuate
CONfirm
proVIDE
acCESS
subMIT
asKEY
manAGE
inVOLVE
reSULT
apPROAL
disCUSS
apPLY
reFER
comPARE
exPLAIN
deMONstrate
SENTENCE STRESS
Delivery & Speech Control in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
CONTENT vs FUNCTION
Content words (nouns, verbs, adj) carry stress · Function words (the, is, to) are reduced
LAUNCH the PRODUCT by
2. Often Heard As
Every word equally stressed — sounds flat and exhausting · Or function words over-stressed, signaling non-fluency
3. Minimal Pairs
WE need a PLAN/we NEED a plan
SEND the REPORT/send THE report
WE launched IT/we LAUNCHED it
THIS is READY/this IS ready
GREAT WORK today/great work TODAY
WHAT do you THINK/what DO you think
I'll FIX the BUG/I'll fix THE bug
MEET on FRIDAY/meet ON friday
NEED more DATA/need MORE data
LET me CHECK/let ME check
4. Clarity Sentences
REVIEW the DATA before the MEETING.
SEND the DRAFT — I'll CHECK it TONIGHT.
THIS APPROACH is FASTER and CHEAPER.
5. Delivery
Stress the word that carries the new information. Reduce everything else.
6. Self-Check
Did the key words stand out clearly from the background words?
50 Workplace Words — High-Stress Content Words vs Function Words
launch
review
deliver
confirm
analyze
present
improve
resolve
achieve
complete
the
a
an
is
are
was
will
to
of
and
strategy
results
deadline
budget
feedback
target
update
decision
meeting
proposal
we
they
our
its
this
that
with
for
from
but
data
goals
clients
teams
issues
risks
actions
changes
progress
output
THOUGHT GROUPS
Pausing for Clarity in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
[ ]   [ ]   [ ]
Group words into meaning units · Pause between groups, not within them · Each group = one breath-thought
Before we begin let me clarify the main goal
2. Often Heard As
Random pauses mid-phrase — "we need to...pause...launch" · Breaks meaning and confuses listener
3. Minimal Pairs
Before we begin / let me clarify/Before / we / begin let / me clarify
The goal / is to grow / our client base/The goal is / to grow our / client base
By Friday / I need / your sign-off/By / Friday I / need your sign-off
We reviewed / the results / this morning/We reviewed the / results this / morning
If you agree / send the draft / today/If you / agree send / the draft today
Based on data / we recommend / moving forward/Based / on data we / recommend moving forward
The team / delivered on time / and on budget/The team delivered / on time / and on budget
Our focus / for this quarter / is retention/Our focus for / this quarter is / retention
Please confirm / the next steps / by end of day/Please confirm the / next steps by / end of day
I'll share / a quick summary / after the call/I'll share a / quick summary after / the call
4. Clarity Sentences
Before we begin / let me outline / the agenda.
The goal / is simple / we need results.
By Friday / I need / your final answer.
5. Delivery
Mark your thought groups before presenting. Pause is not weakness — it's control.
6. Self-Check
Did I pause between ideas, not inside them?
50 Workplace Phrases — Thought Group Practice
before we begin
let me clarify
the main goal
by end of day
as I mentioned
in summary
moving forward
that said
to be clear
in other words
the key issue
our top priority
to wrap up
based on data
going forward
in this quarter
as a result
for that reason
to summarize
at this stage
first of all
in addition
on the other hand
as a follow-up
to confirm
let me explain
in the meantime
for example
that being said
in conclusion
the next step
on that note
with that in mind
to your point
as discussed
for the record
at the same time
from my perspective
to follow up
in short
—
—
—
—
—
—
—
—
—
—
LINKING
Connected Speech in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
C→V     V→V     C→C
Final consonant connects to next vowel · Words flow without gaps in natural speech
pick-it-up turn-it-in all-of-it check-it-out
2. Often Heard As
Each word pronounced separately — sounds choppy and non-native · Breaks natural rhythm
3. Minimal Pairs
pick it up/pick_it_up
turn it in/turn_it_in
all of it/all_of_it
check it out/check_it_out
run it by/run_it_by
sign off on/sign_off_on
wrap it up/wrap_it_up
hold on a/hold_on_a
look at it/look_at_it
think about it/think_about_it
4. Clarity Sentences
Let's check it out and wrap it up before end of day.
Can you run it by the team and sign off on it?
All of it has been taken into account.
5. Delivery
In fluent speech, linked phrases sound like one word. Practice each link slowly.
6. Self-Check
Did words flow into each other without pauses between them?
50 Workplace Phrases — Linking in Connected Speech
pick it up
check it out
run it by
sign off on
wrap it up
turn it in
all of it
look at it
think about
put it off
take it on
work it out
hand it in
talk it over
bring it up
pass it on
call it off
back it up
cut it out
go ahead
go over it
look into it
reach out to
follow up on
weigh in on
focus on it
build on that
pull it off
carry it out
set it up
break it down
lay it out
take it back
move it up
push it back
lock it in
scale it up
roll it out
phase it in
phase it out
map it out
flesh it out
tie it up
clear it up
sort it out
nail it down
drill it down
factor it in
leave it open
close it out
REDUCTION
Natural Speech in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
gonna   wanna   hafta
Function words and auxiliaries reduce in natural speech · Not lazy — it's standard fluent English
going to→gonna want to→wanna have to→hafta could have→coulda
2. Often Heard As
Full formal forms always used — "I am going to review it" in casual contexts · sounds stiff and over-careful
3. Minimal Pairs
going to/gonna
want to/wanna
have to/hafta
got to/gotta
out of/outta
kind of/kinda
sort of/sorta
should have/shoulda
would have/woulda
could have/coulda
4. Clarity Sentences
I'm gonna review the draft — wanna join?
We hafta decide by Friday — shoulda done it sooner.
It's kinda urgent — gotta get it done today.
5. Delivery
Use reduced forms in informal conversations and meetings — not in formal writing.
6. Self-Check
Was my speech level appropriate — natural but not too casual?
50 Common Reduced Forms in Workplace Speech
gonna
wanna
hafta
gotta
kinda
sorta
outta
lotta
oughta
shoulda
woulda
coulda
mighta
mustve
musta
letme
lemme
gimme
dunno
tryna
sposta
supposta
betcha
gotcha
whatcha
howzit
doncha
wouldja
couldja
aintcha
isntit
wasntit
cantit
wouldnt
shouldnt
couldnt
didnt
doesnt
isnt
wasnt
werent
havent
hasnt
hadnt
willnot
wonthave
mightve
—
—
—
PROFESSIONAL PACE
Delivery & Speech Control in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
RATE   PAUSE   RHYTHM
Speed, silence, and rhythm working together · Pace signals confidence and control
slow for key points pause before conclusions speed up for transitions slow for emphasis
2. Often Heard As
Rushing through key information · Or speaking too slowly throughout · Both reduce listener comprehension and confidence
3. Minimal Pairs
Rushed delivery/Controlled delivery
No pauses/Strategic pauses
Flat rhythm/Varied rhythm
Too fast for data/Slow down for numbers
Even pace throughout/Pace shifts signal importance
Filling silence with uh/Owning the silence
Speaking faster when nervous/Slowing when it matters most
Trailing off at end/Landing each sentence
Same speed for all content/Slow for complex ideas
No change for emphasis/Change pace to emphasize
4. Clarity Sentences
This number — [pause] — changed our entire outlook.
Let me slow down here. This part is critical.
[Pause] To summarize: three actions, one deadline.
5. Delivery
Silence is not weakness. Pause before the most important word in every sentence.
6. Self-Check
Did I pause strategically, or just when I needed to breathe?
50 Workplace Situations Requiring Pace Control
presenting data
sharing numbers
key recommendations
critical decisions
change announcements
conflict conversations
performance reviews
strategic updates
project risks
executive briefings
complex explanations
technical details
process changes
policy updates
new directives
budget announcements
restructuring news
team feedback
difficult questions
closing statements
opening statements
transitions between topics
summarizing key points
emphasizing risks
naming next steps
assigning accountability
confirming timelines
gaining alignment
handling objections
asking for approval
reading the room
adjusting to confusion
signaling importance
confirming understanding
wrapping up meetings
introducing agenda items
explaining trade-offs
comparing options
making recommendations
highlighting concerns
acknowledging challenges
celebrating successes
setting expectations
managing ambiguity
driving clarity
establishing authority
building credibility
projecting confidence
managing nerves
owning the room
NUMBERS CLARITY
13 vs 30 · Workplace Clarity Application
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
13 vs 30     14 vs 40
-teen = stress on TEEN · -ty = stress on first syllable · Different stress = different meaning
thirTEEN THIRty fourTEEN FORty
2. Often Heard As
13 and 30 confused in context — financial and scheduling errors · Listener can't distinguish at speed
3. Minimal Pairs
thirTEEN/THIRty
fourTEEN/FORty
fifTEEN/FIFty
sixTEEN/SIXty
sevENTEEN/SEVenty
eighTEEN/EIGHty
nineTEEN/NINEty
13 items/30 items
$14K/$40K
15 days/50 days
4. Clarity Sentences
We need thirteen units — not thirty.
The budget is fourteen thousand — confirm it's not forty.
Deadline is in fifteen days, not fifty.
5. Delivery
Always stress the distinguishing syllable. Repeat or write down numbers in high-stakes moments.
6. Self-Check
Did the TEEN stress land clearly enough to distinguish from -TY?
50 Common Number Words in Workplace Contexts
thirteen
thirty
fourteen
forty
fifteen
fifty
sixteen
sixty
seventeen
seventy
eighteen
eighty
nineteen
ninety
hundred
thousand
million
billion
quarter
percent
half
double
triple
first
second
third
fourth
fifth
tenth
twentieth
thirtieth
fortieth
hundredth
zero
one
two
three
four
five
six
seven
eight
nine
ten
eleven
twelve
twenty
twenty-five
seventy-five
one hundred
NAMES & PROPER NOUNS
Workplace Clarity Application in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
WHO   WHAT   WHERE
Names carry relationship and credibility · Mispronounced names damage trust
ask to confirm repeat it back use it naturally don't avoid it
2. Often Heard As
Avoiding names entirely · Or guessing and mispronouncing · Both damage rapport with clients and colleagues
3. Minimal Pairs
SAY it/AVOID it
ASK to confirm/GUESS and move on
REPEAT it back/Hope you got it right
USE it naturally/Overuse to compensate
Say THEIR name/Substitute with hey
Client name right/Client name wrong
Colleague correct/Colleague avoided
City names clear/City names skipped
Company names precise/Company names mumbled
Product names sharp/Product names rushed
4. Clarity Sentences
Priya, I'd love your take on this before we move on.
Let me confirm — it's Renata, correct? Not Rinata.
We're presenting to Siemens and Takeda next week.
5. Delivery
When unsure: 'I want to make sure I'm saying your name correctly — is it ___?' People remember who tried.
6. Self-Check
Did I use names with confidence, or avoid them?
50 Common Name and Proper Noun Types in Global Workplaces
first name
last name
surname
title
honorific
Mr.
Ms.
Dr.
client name
colleague name
stakeholder
city name
country name
company name
brand name
product name
project name
department name
team name
platform name
software name
vendor name
partner name
region name
office name
division name
headquarters
subsidiary
market name
industry term
conference name
acronym
initiative
framework
methodology
standard
certification
regulation
policy name
program name
prototype
codename
proposal title
initiative label
meeting name
report title
document name
model name
version name
release name
EMAIL & MEETING LANGUAGE
Workplace Clarity Application in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
FORMAL   SEMI-FORMAL   CASUAL
Register matching is clarity · Using wrong level signals misunderstanding of context
per my last email circling back as discussed quick sync
2. Often Heard As
Overly formal in casual contexts · Or too casual in formal ones · Both reduce credibility and connection
3. Minimal Pairs
Pursuant to our discussion/As we discussed
I am writing to inquire/Quick question
Please advise at your earliest convenience/Let me know when you can
I would like to request/Can I ask for
Kindly revert/Please respond
As per the attached/See attached
I trust this finds you well/Hope you're doing well
Herewith please find/Here's the
Do the needful/Please take action
With regards/Best
4. Clarity Sentences
As discussed, I'll send the draft by noon.
Quick question — can we move Thursday's call?
See attached — let me know if you have questions.
5. Delivery
Match the register to the relationship and the context. When in doubt, semi-formal wins.
6. Self-Check
Did my language match the professional context and relationship?
50 Common Email and Meeting Phrases by Register
as discussed
per our call
following up
circling back
looping in
FYI
for your reference
as mentioned
as promised
see attached
quick sync
let's connect
touch base
reach out
ping me
heads up
just checking in
any updates
moving forward
next steps
action items
who owns this
can you confirm
please advise
kindly note
I wanted to flag
gentle reminder
as a reminder
in the meantime
in the interim
to that end
with that said
on a related note
for the record
off the top
full transparency
bottom line
the ask
the takeaway
in summary
to wrap up
does that make sense
any questions
let me know
please revert
awaiting your response
keep me posted
keep me in the loop
please action
over to you
EMPHASIS & CONTRAST
Workplace Clarity Application in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
PITCH   VOLUME   DURATION
Emphasis = louder + longer + higher pitch on the key word · Contrast highlights the difference between two ideas
THIS plan not THAT plan WE decided THEY delayed
2. Often Heard As
No emphasis variation — flat delivery · Listener can't identify what matters · Or emphasis on wrong word — changes meaning
3. Minimal Pairs
THIS plan not that one/this plan NOT that one
WE own this/we OWN this
The RESULTS not the process/the results not THE process
FAST and cheap/fast AND cheap
I SAID Thursday/I said THURSDAY
NOT my decision/not MY decision
The TEAM failed/the team FAILED
SHE approved it/she APPROVED it
ON time not late/on TIME not late
INCREASE not decrease/increase NOT decrease
4. Clarity Sentences
We need speed — not perfection.
The client decides — not the team.
This quarter's results, not last quarter's.
5. Delivery
Identify your contrast word BEFORE speaking. Emphasis is planned, not accidental.
6. Self-Check
Did the listener know exactly which word I was emphasizing and why?
50 Common Contrast and Emphasis Words in Workplace Speech
not
but
however
instead
rather
actually
exactly
specifically
particularly
precisely
clearly
definitely
certainly
absolutely
completely
totally
fully
entirely
only
just
even
also
too
still
already
yet
both
either
neither
whereas
while
although
despite
regardless
nevertheless
nonetheless
in contrast
on the other hand
conversely
alternatively
by contrast
compared to
relative to
unlike
as opposed to
rather than
instead of
more than
less than
better than
CLARITY UNDER PRESSURE
Workplace Clarity Application in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
SLOW   BREATHE   LAND
Pressure degrades pronunciation · Anticipate high-stakes moments · Pre-load the language
slower pace full breath clear landing strong finish
2. Often Heard As
Speed increases under pressure · Pronunciation degrades · Filler words increase · Listener confidence drops
3. Minimal Pairs
I think.../I know that...
Kind of.../Specifically...
Maybe we could.../My recommendation is...
Sorry, I mean.../Let me rephrase that.
Um, so.../The key point is...
I'm not sure but.../Based on the data...
We could maybe.../I recommend...
That's a good question.../Here's what I know...
I'll have to check.../I'll confirm by tomorrow.
Does that make sense?/What questions do you have?
4. Clarity Sentences
My recommendation is clear: Option A, by Friday.
Based on the data, we should proceed with caution.
To confirm: three steps, one owner, one deadline.
5. Delivery
Before high-stakes conversations: slow your breath, slow your pace, finish every sentence fully.
6. Self-Check
Did I maintain the same clarity at the end as at the start?
50 High-Stakes Workplace Communication Moments
executive presentation
board briefing
client proposal
investor pitch
performance review
salary negotiation
conflict resolution
escalation call
crisis update
project approval
scope change
budget defense
technical briefing
cross-cultural meeting
difficult feedback
team announcement
status update
risk presentation
strategy session
product launch
vendor negotiation
hiring interview
promotion conversation
process change
restructuring update
urgent request
deadline pressure
stakeholder alignment
leadership question
critical decision
ambiguous situation
complex question
unexpected pushback
rapid-fire Q&A
hostile question
technical challenge
credibility test
authority challenge
language barrier
cultural sensitivity
data dispute
process objection
role ambiguity
scope creep
missed deadline
project failure
team conflict
resource shortage
competing priorities
high visibility moment
CORRECTION & SELF-REPAIR
Workplace Clarity Application in Professional Communication
CLEAR POD
C
1. Sound Awareness
PAUSE   REPHRASE   CONTINUE
Self-repair is a fluency skill · Skilled speakers catch and correct naturally · Listeners trust speakers who self-monitor
that is to say what I mean is let me rephrase to be more precise
2. Often Heard As
Pushing through without repair · Or over-apologizing for slips · Both reduce credibility — confident self-repair builds it
3. Minimal Pairs
Sorry, I mean.../What I mean to say is...
Uh, no, wait.../Let me rephrase that.
I said X — actually.../To be more precise...
Never mind, so.../Let me clarify —
What I'm trying to say.../What I mean is...
Ignore that, anyway.../That is to say —
Sorry, I confused myself.../Let me come back to that.
Did I say X? I meant Y./To correct myself —
Actually, let me back up./Allow me to restate:
Forget what I said.../To clarify my earlier point:
4. Clarity Sentences
Let me rephrase that — what I mean is: three weeks, not three months.
To be more precise: the cost is $14K, not $40K.
To clarify my earlier point — the deadline is Friday, not Monday.
5. Delivery
Self-repair should be calm and natural — not dramatic. One smooth correction and move on.
6. Self-Check
Did I repair cleanly, without over-apologizing or losing momentum?
50 Common Self-Repair Phrases in Professional Speech
let me rephrase
what I mean is
to be more precise
that is to say
let me clarify
to correct myself
allow me to restate
to put it another way
in other words
more specifically
to be exact
what I meant to say
let me come back to that
to clarify my point
to be clear
to confirm
to be specific
in fact
actually
rather
instead
more accurately
to refine that
a better way to say it
to state it clearly
to be direct
let me restate
going back to
on reflection
upon reflection
to revise that
correcting myself
I should clarify
what I intended
let me be more precise
to be accurate
strictly speaking
to put it simply
to summarize clearly
that said
to add precision
more exactly
upon second thought
if I'm being honest
to be transparent
to state plainly
to say it differently
to say it plainly
to put it precisely
to be forthright
